content.text: Android provides two distinct mechanisms for protecting secrets at rest:

and raw Android Keystore operations. Both ultimately rely on the Android Keystore system for key storage, but they differ in threat model, API surface, and the kind of secrets they protect. Choosing between them is a function of the secret's lifetime, the authentication requirements before it can be used, and the performance characteristics of your app's credential retrieval path.

Never embed the Anthropic Claude API key in an Android binary — neither in code, resources, nor storage. Even hardware-backed Keystore cannot protect a secret that should never be on the device. All Claude API calls must be routed through your backend proxy, which holds the API key server-side and authenticates clients with short-lived session tokens.

attrs.alt: Comparison of EncryptedSharedPreferences vs Android Keystore: features, strengths, limitations, and decision matrix for Claude session tokens, refresh tokens, API keys, and user preferences
attrs.title: EncryptedSharedPreferences vs Android Keystore

content.text: that transparently encrypts keys and values using Google's Tink library. The master key lives in the Android Keystore (not in the prefs file) and is used only to encrypt/decrypt the file-level AES key. Keys are encrypted with AES-256-SIV (deterministic, for stable lookup), and values are encrypted with AES-256-GCM.

generates the master key in the Android Keystore if it does not already exist. Subsequent calls reuse the existing key. The prefs file on disk is unreadable without the Keystore key — but the key itself has no user-authentication gate by default.

, the encrypted prefs file is backed up to Google Drive. The Keystore key is NOT backed up (it is hardware-bound). This means restoring the backup to another device produces a prefs file that cannot be decrypted — and the encrypted tokens are effectively lost. More importantly, if you ever have a device-specific Keystore key and a backed-up prefs file on a different device, the token is unreadable there.

<!-- AndroidManifest.xml -->
<application
    android:allowBackup="false"
    android:dataExtractionRules="@xml/backup_rules"
    ... >

<!-- Or selectively exclude sensitive prefs in res/xml/backup_rules.xml -->
<!-- (API 31+) -->
<cloud-backup>
    <exclude domain="sharedpref" path="claude_session_prefs.xml"/>
</cloud-backup>
Raw Android Keystore for Long-Lived Secrets
When you need a biometric or PIN authentication gate before decrypting a secret — for example, a long-lived OAuth refresh token that allows re-issuing session tokens — use the raw Keystore API with
. The Keystore will not allow the key to be used until the user authenticates.

Android Keystore keys are not arbitrary byte containers — they are cryptographic key references. To protect a string like a refresh token, you encrypt the token bytes with the Keystore-backed AES key and store the ciphertext (+ IV) in regular
or a file. Only the key lives in the Keystore; the encrypted payload lives in normal storage.
unless the user just authenticated. The canonical pattern is to initialize the Cipher, show a
with the Cipher, and decrypt inside the prompt's success callback.

For a refresh token or any secret payload larger than a single key, use the envelope pattern: generate a random Data Encryption Key (DEK) per secret, encrypt the secret with the DEK, then encrypt the DEK with the Keystore-backed Key Encryption Key (KEK). Store the encrypted DEK and the encrypted payload together. Only the KEK lives in hardware; the DEK and payload live in SharedPreferences or a database.

. Android ties the biometric result to this exact Cipher object so that the authentication cannot be replayed against a different operation. Call
callback must clear any stored ciphertext and prompt the user to log in again through your backend to obtain a fresh session token.

Storing the Claude API key client-side.

nor Keystore solves this. An attacker with a rooted device, a debugger, or a dynamic binary instrumentation tool (Frida) can intercept the decrypted value at the point of use. The correct architecture is a backend proxy — the API key never touches the device.
If the user adds a new fingerprint or changes the PIN, Keystore keys with
and delete the encrypted secret, then prompt re-authentication through your backend.
AES-GCM is catastrophically broken if an IV is reused with the same key. Always let
generate a fresh random IV for each encryption, and store it alongside the ciphertext.
Calling Keystore operations on the main thread.

Not excluding the prefs file from cloud backup.

file backed up to Google Drive cannot be decrypted on another device (the Keystore key is not backed up). At best this silently loses the session token; at worst it exposes which prefs files exist. Always set
or use a backup exclusion rule.
that encrypts both keys and values. Use it for short-lived session tokens returned by your backend proxy. Disable cloud backup for the prefs file.
Raw Android Keystore gives hardware-backed key storage with an optional biometric/PIN auth gate (
). Use it for long-lived refresh tokens that unlock new sessions. The Keystore stores only the encryption key — the ciphertext lives separately.

For secrets larger than a single key, use the envelope pattern: encrypt the secret with a per-secret DEK, then encrypt the DEK with the Keystore-backed KEK. Store encrypted DEK + IV alongside the encrypted payload.

Neither mechanism protects against a sophisticated attacker with code execution in your process. The Claude API key must live server-side — always route through a backend proxy. What you protect client-side are short-lived session tokens, not API keys.
, and never reuse a GCM initialization vector.
